question: Explain the Android Activity lifecycle.
answer:
Activity is the entry point for a screen. The system drives it through callbacks:

• onCreate(savedInstanceState) — Activity created. Inflate UI, restore state. Called ONCE per Activity instance.
• onStart — Activity becomes visible.
• onResume — Activity in foreground, receiving input.
• onPause — losing focus (another Activity in front, multi-window). Pause animations, save anything ephemeral. Called BEFORE onStop and BEFORE the next Activity's onResume.
• onStop — no longer visible.
• onRestart — coming back from stopped (after onStop, before onStart).
• onDestroy — Activity being torn down. Last call. Configuration changes recreate the Activity.
• onSaveInstanceState(out) — pre-onStop hook to save UI state into a Bundle (rotation / process death recovery).

Fragments mirror this with their own callbacks (`onAttach → onCreate → onCreateView → onViewCreated → onStart → onResume → onPause → onStop → onDestroyView → onDestroy → onDetach`).

question: What is a Fragment? When do you use Fragments vs Activities?
answer:
A Fragment is a reusable, self-contained piece of UI that lives inside an Activity (or another Fragment). Has its own lifecycle, view tree, and back stack support.

Use Fragments when:
• Multi-pane layouts (master/detail on tablet vs single-pane on phone) — swap fragments in/out.
• Multiple screens behind a single Activity — common with `Navigation` library.
• Reusable UI components needing their own lifecycle hooks (e.g. a permissions flow).

In modern apps with Jetpack Navigation, often the entire app uses ONE Activity + many Fragments (or, with Compose, one Activity + composable destinations and no Fragments at all).

Gotchas: Fragments have TWO lifecycles (the fragment + its view) — observe `viewLifecycleOwner` for view-bound state, `this` for fragment-bound state.

question: What are Intents? Explicit vs implicit Intents and common use cases.
answer:
An Intent is a message describing an operation to perform. Used to:
• Start an Activity (`startActivity`, `startActivityForResult` / Activity Result API).
• Start / bind a Service.
• Send a Broadcast.

Two flavors:
• Explicit — you specify the exact component (`Intent(ctx, ProfileActivity::class.java)`). Used for in-app navigation.
• Implicit — you specify an action (e.g. `Intent.ACTION_VIEW` + URI). Android picks an app that can handle it. Used for sharing, dialing, opening links.

Intents carry data via extras (Bundle). Modern Android prefers the Activity Result API (`registerForActivityResult`) over the deprecated `startActivityForResult` — it's lifecycle-safe.

question: How do you handle configuration changes (rotation) on Android?
answer:
By default, configuration changes (rotation, locale, dark-mode flip, multi-window resize) trigger Activity destruction + recreation. Strategies to keep state:

• ViewModel — survives configuration changes; tied to the ViewModelStoreOwner (Activity / Fragment / NavBackStackEntry). Idiomatic place for screen state.
• `onSaveInstanceState(out: Bundle)` — small Bundle for transient UI state (selected tab, scroll, draft text). Used after process death too.
• `rememberSaveable` (Compose) — wraps the same mechanism for composables.
• `android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize"` — opts out of recreation. Use sparingly: you must handle layout updates manually and miss the recreation flow.
• Saved State Module (`SavedStateHandle` in ViewModel) — persists key/values that survive process death AND rotation.

question: What is the AndroidManifest and what goes in it?
answer:
AndroidManifest.xml describes your app to the system. Required, located at `app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml`. Contains:

• Application package, version, theme, icon.
• Components — `<activity>`, `<service>`, `<receiver>`, `<provider>`.
• Intent filters — declare which intents each component handles (deep links, share, launchers).
• Permissions — `<uses-permission>` (and runtime requests for dangerous ones).
• Hardware/software requirements (`<uses-feature>` like camera, NFC).
• Backup and data extraction rules.
• Network security config (cleartext traffic, certificate pinning).

Gradle merges multiple manifests (main, build variants, libraries) into a final manifest at build time — see `build/intermediates/merged_manifests/.../AndroidManifest.xml`.
code_example:
<!-- AndroidManifest.xml — main pieces -->
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">

  <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />
  <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.CAMERA" />
  <uses-feature android:name="android.hardware.camera" android:required="false" />

  <application
    android:name=".MyApp"
    android:allowBackup="true"
    android:icon="@mipmap/ic_launcher"
    android:roundIcon="@mipmap/ic_launcher_round"
    android:theme="@style/Theme.MyApp"
    android:networkSecurityConfig="@xml/network_security_config">

    <activity
      android:name=".HomeActivity"
      android:exported="true">
      <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
      </intent-filter>
    </activity>

    <!-- Deep link -->
    <activity android:name=".ProfileActivity" android:exported="true">
      <intent-filter android:autoVerify="true">
        <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" />
        <data android:scheme="https"
              android:host="example.com"
              android:pathPrefix="/u/" />
      </intent-filter>
    </activity>

    <service android:name=".SyncService" android:exported="false" />
    <receiver android:name=".BootReceiver" android:exported="true">
      <intent-filter><action android:name="android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED"/></intent-filter>
    </receiver>

    <provider
      android:name="androidx.core.content.FileProvider"
      android:authorities="${applicationId}.fileprovider"
      android:exported="false"
      android:grantUriPermissions="true">
      <meta-data android:name="android.support.FILE_PROVIDER_PATHS" android:resource="@xml/file_paths"/>
    </provider>
  </application>
</manifest>

question: How do runtime permissions work on Android?
answer:
Android splits permissions into:
• Normal — granted at install time (network, vibrate). Just declare in Manifest.
• Dangerous — privacy-sensitive (camera, location, contacts). Must be declared AND requested at runtime starting Android 6 (API 23).
• Special — system-managed (overlay, accessibility). Require the user to flip a Settings toggle.

Flow:
1) Declare in manifest.
2) Check at runtime with `ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission`.
3) If not granted, request via Activity Result API (`ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission` or `RequestMultiplePermissions`).
4) Show rationale (`shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale`) before re-asking.
5) Handle permanent denials by deep-linking to app settings.

Android 13+ adds POST_NOTIFICATIONS as a dangerous permission. Android 14+ adds partial photo selection.
